author: @johnbrizendine7716
text:
2 stroke engines are the easiest to convert to steam power, but you have to introduce hygroscopic oil into the engine with the steam/hot air/air pressure/etc. to keep the bottom end lubricated, but will work with almost no conversion at all due to the design of it.
When using an air compressor or converting an internal combustion 4 stroke engine to steam, you have to keep a close watch on the oil since the steam will condense back to water in the crank case.
I had a steam engine I built out of a 60cc weed whacker engine and it performed really well, especially when I used waste oil to boil the water for it, and I just had a setup that would add hygroscopic oil into the intake with the steam and it ran almost continuously for about a year without any issues, until I decided to save the waste oil for filtering and burning in my diesel truck and car.

author: @shortbuslife3440
text:
You missed probably the simplest valves of them all which would probably work fantastically for a steam engine and that's the reed valve and  the ball valve. No controllers or timing to worry about and probably the most reliable plus it follows the KISS principle.
For the ball valve buy a non return valve plumbing fitting and remove the plastic internals and the spring and remake them with brass unless you can buy all metal version, the reed valve would be a bit more tricky to find or make.

author: @rogueart7706
text:
Be aware that your crank is an opposed stroke. 4 stroke twins are usually simultaneous stroke meaning both pistons move together so that the firing pattern is every 360 degrees apart. 
The one in your video is like a John Deere model B where the pistons move opposite and firing is at 360 then 540degrees. 
There’s nothing wrong with that…opposed piston movement cancels each other out and is more balanced, that’s why John Deere built it that way. 
Your engine will have the John Deere “plop” sound instead of a modern Kohler sound. 
I’m building a cross plane 4 cylinder right now which is like 2 John Deeres 90 degrees apart. 
Personally I like the John Deere “plop” sound 👍

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Annother valve type... Pneumatic or hydraulic. Pretty much a piston that highish pressure fluid presses down on to open say a poppet valve. But can get ones that handle fluid logic. Including say a flip flop. 
A nut or some other projection on side of flywheel could be use to trigger a pneumatic sensor... Akin to a microswitch, but outputting pressured air... 
The output from one such sensor triggers a pneumatic flip flop to latch "outputing pressure"
Output from 2nd such sensor triggers flip flop to latch into "no output pressure."
Output from from flip flip. Drives main cylinder valve.  

Connection between pneumatic sensor and flip flop can be flexible hose... 
Adjust position of pneumatic sensors around flywheel to adjust timing of when valve opens, and independently when it shuts.

Functionality should be same as a trip valve, ( the bump valve is a simple none adjustable form of such) with less of the mechanical issues associated with them.

Rebuilding an engine to run on another type of fuel is a good challenge to get right and running correctly. I have another challenge for you. Getting one engine to run on different types of fuel without having to do a full teardown rebiuld. author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: That is a great explanation of the differences with example!  I would highly recommend the slide type valve from steam engines as the way to go.  You have two pistons and the simple slide valve I have seen is an eccentric from the flywheel that moves a block to either end of a solid steam piston.  In your case, instead of switching between left and right sides of one piston, you could have it switch which piston and have a second block on another eccentric 180 degrees from the first to act as the exhaust valves.  That would allow one input of compressed air or steam and one outlet with the added benefit that you could accidentally swap them and the only result would be running in reverse direction.

author: @C-M-E
text:
With a certain dip into irony, I was looking at turning a two stroke into an on-demand air compressor before scoring a big ol' honker (tank-based 5hp compressor that is) out of the modern day classified ads just recently, figuring the relatively simple reed valves used on most two strokes would be the easiest way to convert to what I needed. After digging through an awful lot of schematics of various air compressor pumps to see how feasible it would be, even the really expensive models still operate with very simplistic reed valve systems. 
I'm rather excited to see what you come up with, Robert!  Outside of maybe a change in gasket material, that air compressor pump should be a real treat for an externally-produced steam receptacle.  Valve controls and pressure relief systems are really the heart of any good steam engine, so don't skimp on those. 👍

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: Would doing a stirling out of something like that be more trouble than its worth, do you think?

replies.author: @peoplesresearchcenter
replies.text: Ringbom type Stirling would probably be the easiest conversion. Of course, Stirling engines don't require valves of any kind. Just the external application of heat.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it would be fine - you can't do a good job with a inline set of pistons they share the same cylinder block and you really want to keep the hot and old side separate - that's why V twins are popular

replies.author: @peoplesresearchcenter
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering The Ringbom type Stirling can overcome that issue by having the heat exchangers completely external to the power cylinder(s)

author: @yaka2490
text: ooo i am currently working with replications of the correct Nikola Tesla Valvular Conduit and can run at 1000's of pulses per second as it was designed to work with a PDE so no reason it cant be made to work with any combustion engine. robert and these things are amazing once you fully understand the setup and can tune them hehe they will last for ever with no moving parts as well. They use them in the space industry for good reason as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have been pondering that mate - but what really stumps me is an easy way to make it - I am still mulling it over - can you keep me in touch with how it goes?

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering a YouTuber called Integza has used a ceramic resin to print cylindrical Tesla valves for a rocket engine.  Could use the ceramic resin to test the concept and refine the design, then have the final design printed in metal by a service.

replies.author: @yaka2490
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah it's a tricky thing to do bud I have an elegoo saturn which with my new cad skills I can build and print these simply. I will keep you posted with test data hopefully in the not too distant future.

replies.author: @yaka2490
replies.text: @McRootbeer be carful of others trying to replicate these valves as imo there not direct replications and i am not sure they fully understand them.

author: @FasterLower
text:
Nice article BUT a little simplistic. Changes into SOME engine types cannot be done with just a head change. How do you change an aircompressor into a 2 stroke petrol engine by only changing the cylinder head?
Also you failed to mention reed valves (were common on small 2 stroke engines) and sleeve valves (Bristol Centaurus - Hawker Fury/Sea Fury)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hmmm - I don't want to seem rude mate - but even though it is simple you might have missed the point - we are not changing the head - we are working on the head to change the valve - and I didn't forget those valve types I assure you lol

replies.author: @newmonengineering
replies.text: A flame licker is a 2 stroke engine with only a single valve and would be fairly simple to modify any 4 cycle to be a 2 cycle flame licker. Of course they are a crude engine and probably not the best design but it goes to show that 2 cycle or 4 cycle are still just a valve combination difference.

replies.author: @FasterLower
replies.text: Thank you chaps, I stand corrected.

replies.author: @peoplesresearchcenter
replies.text: Actually most hot air (Stirling type) engines don't use any valves, just heat exchangers.

replies.author: @dr.froghopper6711
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I share his feelings but I confess that I enjoy your ingenuity. We are surrounded by technology today that is being thrown away and it’s astonishing to me because 250-300 years ago, it would have been seen as miraculous. I truly appreciate that you share with us hands on examples of practical engineering that is available to the rest of us. You trigger new ideas and thinking beyond the bounds of yon confining box!

author: @bencapobianco2045
text:
This video is right up my alley I am a machinist and have been looking to do something like this for more years than I care to admit I would love to see it done with an Arduino and solenoid valves would be really cool to run that on steam with programmable timing to get the best efficiency out of it. Awesome content as always love the channel. 
Just another quick thought if it runs on steam maybe a boiler using a Fresnel lens in the sun?

replies.text: Yep, join the club mate..
